timeline.event: Born at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.
Studied at the Reading Abbey Girls' School in Reading, Berkshire.
Started writing novels at the age of 14.
Wrote the epistolary novel 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility' based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Dedicated 'Emma' to the Prince Regent (later George IV) shortly before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, leaving her bedridden for long periods.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 while writing 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Born in Pentonville, London, England.
Began learning Greek.
Began learning Latin, Euclid, and algebra, and acted as a tutor for his younger siblings.
Reached the level of being able to read original texts of Plato and Demosthenes.
Started studying scholastic logic and original texts of Aristotle's logic.
Began studying political economy.
Stayed in France for a year and took courses in chemistry, zoology, and logic at the Faculty of Sciences in Montpellier.
Suffered from a nervous breakdown.
Founded the Utilitarian Society with his friends.
Published 'A System of Logic'.
Published 'Essays on Some Unsettled Questions of Political Economy'.
Published 'Principles of Political Economy'.
Married Harriet Taylor.
Harriet Taylor died, and he retired from the East India Company.
Published 'On Liberty'.
Published 'Utilitarianism' and 'Considerations on Representative Government'.
Elected as Lord Rector of the University of St Andrews and elected to the House of Commons as a Member of Parliament for the City of Westminster.
Term as Member of Parliament ended.
Became the first person in Parliament to advocate for women's right to vote, and published 'The Subjection of Women'.
Died in Avignon, France, and 'Autobiography' was published.
Published 'Three Essays on Religion'.
Published 'Chapters on Socialism'.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the System of Logic
keyAchievements.description: Published 'A System of Logic' in 1843, completing Bacon's inductive logic and presenting a groundbreaking methodology for natural science research.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Liberal Political Theory
keyAchievements.description: Left behind a classic work on the concept of liberty through 'On Liberty', presenting the basic principles of modern liberal democracy by defending freedom of expression as a fundamental right of citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Utilitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Developed Bentham's utilitarianism, his godfather and teacher, into his own system by adding Saint-Simonianism and Romanticism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Rights and Social Reform
keyAchievements.description: As a Member of Parliament, he advocated for various social reforms including women's rights, proportional representation, labor unions, and cooperative organization of agriculture, and was the first to argue for women's suffrage in Parliament.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Market Socialism Theory
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the early theorists of market socialism for advocating the replacement of capitalist enterprises with worker cooperatives.

faq.question: How was John Stuart Mill educated?
faq.answer: He was educated primarily by his father, James Mill, and was a precocious child, learning Greek at the age of three and Latin and advanced mathematics at eight.

faq.question: What were Mill's views on religion?
faq.answer: He was critical of religion itself, never having had religious beliefs, denied Christian doctrines, and viewed religion as the greatest enemy of freedom.

faq.question: How did Harriet Taylor influence John Stuart Mill?
faq.answer: Harriet profoundly influenced Mill's thoughts, particularly strengthening his advocacy for women's rights, and influenced the writing of 'On Liberty' and 'The Subjection of Women'.

faq.question: What did John Stuart Mill think about the 'stationary state'?
faq.answer: He believed that a stationary state of capital and wealth might not be terrible but could actually be a much more advanced and happier state than the present, anticipating that humanity would be less concerned with money and focus more on values like dignity, integrity, and justice.

faq.question: Was John Stuart Mill a socialist?
faq.answer: He called himself a socialist and is considered one of the early market socialist theorists for advocating the replacement of capitalist businesses with worker cooperatives.

timeline.event: Born in a rural French village.
Met Jacques Biétrix de Rozières and entered into a long romantic relationship.
In the year her biological father died, she began her career as a public intellectual and wrote the play 'Zamore and Mirza', an anti-slavery work.
Published the pamphlet 'Letters to the People'.
'Zamore and Mirza' was not performed; wrote another play titled 'The Black Market'.
Became a member of the 'Social Club' or 'Society of the Friends of Truth' and began writing the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'.
The Jacobins arrested and imprisoned her allies, members of the Girondins.
The Girondin members were executed at the guillotine.
Was sentenced to death by the Jacobins.
Executed.
An intersection in Paris was named 'Olympe de Gouges' in her honor.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Suffrage
keyAchievements.description: Made an innovative argument during the French Revolution that women should also be granted the right to vote.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen'
keyAchievements.description: Drafted a declaration articulating the equal rights of women in 1791 out of concern that the French Constitution failed to address women's suffrage.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-Slavery Activism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for the rights of Black people, writing the anti-slavery play 'Zamore and Mirza' in 1784 and 'The Black Market' in 1789.

keyAchievements.title: Proposals for Marriage Contracts and Protection of Women
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for equal division of wealth between husband and wife, equal property division in case of divorce, and laws to protect impoverished women and their children.

keyAchievements.title: Defense of the Monarchy
keyAchievements.description: Opposed the abolition of the monarchy during the French Revolution and took a stance defending King Louis XVI and Queen Marie Antoinette.

faq.question: Who was Olympe de Gouges?
faq.answer: Olympe de Gouges was an innovative civic activist and a radical advocate for women's rights who argued that women should also be given suffrage during the French Revolution.

faq.question: What is her most famous work?
faq.answer: Her most famous work is the 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen', written in 1791.

faq.question: Why was she executed?
faq.answer: She was executed in 1793 by the Jacobins on charges of inappropriate behavior and attempting to restore the monarchy, citing the contents of her flyer 'The Three Urns, or the Salvation of the Fatherland, by an Aerial Traveler' and her unfinished play 'France Saved, or the Tyrant Dethroned'.

faq.question: What was her stance on the rights of Black people?
faq.answer: She spoke out against black slavery by writing the script for the anti-slavery play 'Zamore and Mirza' in 1784 and writing 'The Black Market' in 1789.

faq.question: What was her stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She welcomed the French Revolution but grew disillusioned that the rights of equality it championed were not extended to women. She opposed the revolutionaries' attempts to abolish the monarchy and supported its preservation.

timeline.event: Henrik Johan Ibsen was born in Skien, Norway.
At age 18, had a son, Hans Jacob Hendrichsen Birkedalen, from a relationship with Else Sophie Jensdatter Birkedalen.
Published his first play, 'Catiline', under the pseudonym 'Brynjolf Bjarme', and had his first stage production, 'The Burial Mound'.
Became the artistic director of the Christiania Theater and married Suzannah Thoresen.
Son Sigurd was born.
Left Christiania for Sorrento, Italy, beginning 27 years of living abroad.
Published the play 'Brand', which brought him critical acclaim and financial success.
Published the play 'Peer Gynt', for which Edvard Grieg composed the incidental music.
Moved from Italy to Dresden, Germany.
Published his only collection of poetry, 'Poems' (Digte).
Wrote 'Emperor and Galilean', dramatizing the life of the Roman Emperor Julian the Apostate, and was knighted.
Moved to Munich and began work on his first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society'.
His first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society', was published and first performed.
Published the play 'A Doll's House', which brought him international recognition.
Published the play 'Ghosts', containing commentary on the morality of Ibsen's society.
Published the play 'An Enemy of the People', addressing controversial themes.
Published the play 'The Wild Duck', considered by many to be Ibsen's finest work.
Published the later work 'Hedda Gabler', exploring psychological conflict.
Returned to Christiania, Norway, after 27 years of living abroad.
Published the later work 'The Master Builder', exploring psychological conflict, and was made a Commander.
Received the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Olav.
Suffered several strokes.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Died at his home in Christiania at the age of 78.
The 'Ibsen Year' was celebrated in Norway and many other countries to commemorate the 100th anniversary of his death, and 'A Doll's House' was the world's most performed play.
The 'Delhi Ibsen Festival' began to be held annually in Delhi, India.
Håkon Anton Fagerås created two bronze busts of Ibsen to be installed in Ibsen Park in Sorrento, Italy, and in the Skien municipality.
A marble statue of Ibsen was created for the Ibsen Museum in Oslo.
Google featured a Doodle celebrating Henrik Ibsen's 185th birthday.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Modern Drama
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and referred to as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered Realistic Theater
keyAchievements.description: Though he also wrote lyrical epic works, he pioneered realistic theater.

keyAchievements.title: World's Most Performed Playwright
keyAchievements.description: He is the most frequently performed playwright in the world after Shakespeare.

keyAchievements.title: International Recognition
keyAchievements.description: Achieved international recognition with works such as 'Brand', 'Peer Gynt', and 'A Doll's House'.

keyAchievements.title: Redefined the Rules of Drama
keyAchievements.description: Completely rewrote the rules of drama through realism.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Playwright
keyAchievements.description: Influenced figures such as George Bernard Shaw, Oscar Wilde, and James Joyce.

faq.question: When was Henrik Ibsen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born on March 20, 1828, and died on May 23, 1906.

faq.question: How is Henrik Ibsen evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: He is considered the world's greatest playwright of the 19th century and is known as the 'Father of Modern Drama'.

faq.question: What are Henrik Ibsen's representative works?
faq.answer: His works include 'A Doll's House', 'Ghosts', 'An Enemy of the People', 'The Wild Duck', and 'Peer Gynt'.

faq.question: Did Henrik Ibsen win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: He was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1902, 1903, and 1904.

faq.question: What were Henrik Ibsen's political views?
faq.answer: He expressed anarchistic views, stating that the state is a curse upon the individual and should be abolished.

timeline.event: Born into a noble Prussian family in Berlin.
Began his education after his father died.
Educated by a tutor alongside his older brother.
Graduated from the Freiberg School of Mines and became a civil servant in the Prussian Department of Mines.
Toured rock salt mining regions in various Central European countries.
Inherited a large fortune upon his mother's death.
Began his expedition to Spanish America with the support of the Spanish Prime Minister.
Returned from South America.
Published 23 volumes of 'Personal Narrative of Travels to the Equinoctial Regions of the New Continent'.
Published 'Views of Nature' (Ansichten der Natur) in Berlin.
Demonstrated the idea and method of comparing climatic conditions across different countries through the 'Creation of Isothermal Lines'.
Taught physical geography at the University of Vienna.
Died at the age of 90 and was honored with a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Kosmos'
keyAchievements.description: After observing physical geography and ecology during his expeditions to South America and Central Asia, he compiled vast amounts of data to write 'Kosmos', a masterpiece regarded as a monumental work in modern geography.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Geography
keyAchievements.description: Became a founder of modern geography along with Carl Ritter.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Humboldt Current
keyAchievements.description: The ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru was named the Humboldt Current in honor of his investigations.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundations for Physical Geography and Geophysics
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundations of physical geography and geophysics through his investigations of the mountain ranges of tropical America.

keyAchievements.title: Observation and Study of Meteor Showers
keyAchievements.description: Conducted observations of meteor showers from Tenerife in the Canary Islands; his research on their periodicity became a foundation for modern astronomical observation.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Views of Nature'
keyAchievements.description: Investigated and studied Mount Vesuvius in Italy and published 'Views of Nature' in Berlin in 1807.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Isothermal Lines
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated the method of comparing climatic conditions of various countries in 'Des Lignes Isothermes' (1817) and discovered the rate of temperature decrease with increasing altitude.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Earth's Magnetism
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the strength of the Earth's magnetic field decreases from the poles toward the equator.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of International Magnetic Observatories and Meteorological Institute
keyAchievements.description: Created an international network of magnetic observatories and established a meteorological institute in Prussia.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Specimen Collection and Research
keyAchievements.description: Investigated the relationship between plants and their environment, collecting a massive herbarium of 60,000 specimens, which included thousands of new species and genera.

faq.question: Who was Alexander von Humboldt?
faq.answer: He was a German geographer, natural scientist, naturalist, and explorer, who along with Carl Ritter, became a founder of modern geography.

faq.question: What is his representative work?
faq.answer: His magnum opus is 'Kosmos', based on data from his expeditions in South America and Central Asia, which is regarded as a monumental achievement in modern geography.

faq.question: How is the Humboldt Current related to him?
faq.answer: The Humboldt Current was named in his honor to commemorate his investigation of the ocean current flowing along the coast of Peru.

faq.question: In what fields did he leave a legacy?
faq.answer: He made tremendous contributions to a variety of fields including flora and fauna, astronomy, climate, mineralogy, and oceanography, in addition to geography, natural science, natural history, and exploration.
